speaker: interviewer
text: Today is November 16, 1995. The interview is with Toby Fluek nee Knobel. Interviewer is Tina Tito. We're in Woodside, New York in the United States of America. The language will be English. Today is November 16, 1995. I am Tina Tito. I'm the interviewer. I will be interviewing Toby Fluek nee Knobel. We're in Woodside Queens, New York in the United States. Mrs. Fluek, could you please tell us your full name and spell it?

speaker: interviewee
text: My name is Toby Fluek, T-O-B-Y F-L-U-E-K.

speaker: interviewer
text: Your maiden name?

speaker: interviewee
text: My maiden is Knobel, K-N-O-B-E-L.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where were you born?

speaker: interviewee
text: I was born in Czernica.

speaker: interviewer
text: Could you spell for us?

speaker: interviewee
text: C-Z-E-R-N-I-A.

speaker: interviewer
text: N-I-C-A? Czernica?

speaker: interviewee
text: Czernica, N-I-C-A, right.

speaker: interviewer
text: What country?

speaker: interviewee
text: Poland.

speaker: interviewer
text: And when were you born, what year?

speaker: interviewee
text: I was born in 1926.

speaker: interviewer
text: Month and date?

speaker: interviewee
text: February 20.

speaker: interviewer
text: Can you tell me a bit about your father, his full name first? Naftali, N-A-F-T-A-L-I, Knobel. He was a quiet man. He was a farmer.

speaker: interviewee
text: And he was sort of sickly. He always walked around with a little [NON-ENGLISH] on his forehead. You know, it's like a wet compress. But he did go to work in the fields. We used to hire help to work in the fields. So he used to sort of supervise.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was your father born in Czernica?

speaker: interviewee
text: He was born in Czernica, yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did he have family in Czernica?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, he had a sister.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was her name?

speaker: interviewee
text: Her name was-- I don't believe it-- Matl, Matl. M-A-T-L. Matl Westler. She was married to Mordche Westler.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did they live near you?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. The village was small. So it was walking distance.

speaker: interviewer
text: How small was the village? What was the population?

speaker: interviewee
text: There was about 250 families.

speaker: interviewer
text: How many Jews?

speaker: interviewee
text: 10 Jewish families.

speaker: interviewer
text: And did your brother-in-law work with your father in the farming? Or, did he have another profession?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. He-- he didn't do any farming. And he used to make a living buying eggs from the peasants and selling them to the city merchants.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was your grandfather also in farming? Is that how your father--

speaker: interviewee
text: No. My grandfather had [NON-ENGLISH], we called it in Yiddish, [NON-ENGLISH], which is like an inn. You know, he was selling whiskey to the farmers.

speaker: interviewer
text: So your father bought up this land to-- to farm it?

speaker: interviewee
text: My father lived in Czernica. My grandparents lived in Czipil. This was like about 15 miles away from Czernica.

speaker: interviewer
text: Could you spell the name that town?

speaker: interviewee
text: Czipil, it's C-Z-I-P-I-L.

speaker: interviewer
text: And where was this area of Czernica in Poland?

speaker: interviewee
text: Czernica was in the region of Lvov, the larger city. It was near Brody, but [INAUDIBLE].

speaker: interviewer
text: So a bit Northeast of Lvov, a little bit going north?

speaker: interviewee
text: I couldn't--

speaker: interviewer
text: OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: --remember that map.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you have a lake in your area near your town?

speaker: interviewee
text: We had a river. The name was Ikva [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] We used to wash the clothing there in the river. We used to hire a woman. A Polish woman used to come a few times during the year to do our laundry. She washed it in a wash tub. And she had to warm the water on the stove. We had a wood-burning stove. And then she rinsed it in the river.

speaker: interviewer
text: And your mother, your mother's full name?

speaker: interviewee
text: My mother was called at home [? Genendal ?] [? Genendal ?] Knobel.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was her maiden name?

speaker: interviewee
text: Her maiden name was Schwartz. And my mother was more of a businesswoman, you know? She-- she tried to handle here and there. She used to buy fruit from the farmers in summer times. And they stored it in one of our rooms. The whole room was full of apples, you know. And then the merchants came and bought it. And--

speaker: interviewer
text: Was your mother also from this town?

speaker: interviewee
text: My mother was born in Czipil, yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: How did they meet? Do you happen to know?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, they were-- a matchmaker. There was-- he was a peddler. He was driving from one town to the other. And he new my mother and her parents. And he knew my father. And that's how he made a [INAUDIBLE]. [LAUGHS] And my mother came to see my father. And she didn't want to marry somebody with a beard. My mother was more modern. [LAUGHS] And then after a while, the parents talked her into it. I think--

speaker: interviewer
text: Tell me about her parents.

speaker: interviewee
text: This came up that my mother's two brothers were in America already at the time. And my mother said she doesn't want to get married, because the rest of the family will leave. And she will be left behind.

speaker: interviewer
text: So my mother was telling me that story that my grandmother said to her, I will never leave you. I promise you. You know, they wanted to marry off a daughter. That was a big thing to marry off a daughter. So she got married. And when my mother had two children, my older two sisters, there was the mass emigration. And my uncles and aunt wanted to go to America. So, of course, the grandparents they all left together. So the grandparents went to America. This was around 19--

speaker: interviewee
text: That was in 1920- '21. And they promised my mother that they will bring her to America. So then they found out that my mother could come with her two children. But she couldn't bring her husband, my father. She had to come here, stay here for five years and get-- become a citizen. And then my father could come. So my mother didn't want to separate with my father. And that's how she stayed behind.

speaker: interviewer
text: So in a sense, you and your siblings did not know your grandparents?

speaker: interviewee
text: I didn't know my--

speaker: interviewer
text: Or aunts and uncles, you've never met them, you had never met them?

speaker: interviewee
text: I never met them until I came here to the United States, yes--

speaker: interviewer
text: You said you had--

speaker: interviewer
text: You said you had two siblings? You had-- you had three-- three other--

speaker: interviewee
text: I had. But when my-- I'm talking in the '20s, my two sisters were little children. My brother was born in 1923.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was his name?

speaker: interviewee
text: Aron, A-R-O-N. And--

speaker: interviewer
text: And your sisters' names?

speaker: interviewee
text: My oldest sister was Sarah. And my middle sister was Lajcie, Lea, Lea actually. [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] And we were happy in the village. And we didn't know any better. [LAUGHS] I went to school there, to elementary school. And I had a lonely childhood actually. Because there weren't too many Jewish families. And I was the only child, actually, my age. My sisters had friends. They were older, her age. My cousins-- Matl's two daughters-- she was friends with. And my age, there were-- there were no children. So I had a lonely childhood.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was your relationship with your sisters and brothers? Describe--

speaker: interviewee
text: I was close-- I wasn't that close with my older sister. Because she was 16 years older than me. I was close with my middle sister. And my oldest sister also went away to learn dressmaking. I remember when I was a little girl that my sister came back. And-- and there was such a fuss about her. And as I could remember, I didn't know who she was. She must have been away like for two years or something. And I didn't know. And nobody bothered to explain to the child, well, your sister is back or something. So they were just pushing be away. [LAUGHS] I shouldn't be in the way. So I realize now that my sister came back probably from out of town. She went to learn dressmaking.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you know where she went?

speaker: interviewee
text: I don't. No. I just remember that incident. I was close, very close with my brother. But my brother, he was of school age. He went to [INAUDIBLE]. And he was boarding there to attend cheder. We had no cheder in our village. The previous years, like in the '20s, there were about 30 Jewish families in our village. So they had a Hebrew teacher, a rabbi, you know, that taught the children. They had a cheder. But when I was growing up, many people left the village. Some went to America. Some went to France to work. And some moved out to the cities. So I was the only child there.

speaker: interviewer
text: So your village, you felt the immigration, the emigration from Poland?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. We-- we knew that people are going to America. Yeah. In fact, when I was growing up, it was more difficult to go. There was a quota already then. The mass immigration was in the '20s. And in the '30s, it was more difficult to go to America. If fact, my sister was very anxious to go to America. So I remember there was talk that there was a-- a quota. And you couldn't go easily.

speaker: interviewer
text: Now, your older sister, did she have anything that she did, any work that she did?

speaker: interviewee
text: She did dressmaking. She made a living dressmaking.

speaker: interviewer
text: In the house, did she have her own?

speaker: interviewee
text: In the house, we had three rooms. So like I said, the second room was used for-- [LAUGHS] for keeping the apples [INAUDIBLE], We also had rented that room. A teacher lived from elementary school teacher, we rented out a room. Or, we had a bar there for a few years, various things.

speaker: interviewer
text: So we lived in one room, one bedroom, one large room and a kitchen. So in that one large room, we had a-- a [NON-ENGLISH] in one corner, the services for Saturday held there and the holidays. And then the machine was in another corner.

speaker: interviewee
text: And the two beds were on-- on the second corner. And then for my two sisters, a bed-- to make a bed, they used four chairs and two benches. And that's how the bed was made every night, for them with a straw sack.

speaker: interviewer
text: So everything was done in that very same room. Yes. I remember Saturday morning, my mother used to wake me up. We have to make the bed. Get up. Because they're coming to the services soon.

speaker: interviewee
text: By the way, I slept-- when I was a little girl, I slept with my mother. And I remember, I must have been like five years old or something, four or five. And when my brother was little, he slept with his father, with my father. And later, we used-- we sort of used at times, we used the second-- the second room. So I remember sleeping in the second room some-- for some time. And like I said, we slept on this made-up bed from chairs and benches. [LAUGHS]

speaker: interviewer
text: What did your second sister-- your middle sister-- do?

speaker: interviewee
text: My middle sister just helped out in the house. She helped out my mother in the kitchen and cleaning and so forth.

speaker: interviewer
text: And your brother was away in cheder?

speaker: interviewee
text: And my brother was at cheder. My middle sister was sort of-- we always used to call her-- she was the [NON-ENGLISH], you know? She was sickly. She had some kind of disease when she was a little girl. And she was left sort of slow, you know.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you have a doctor in the village?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. We didn't have a doctor.

speaker: interviewer
text: What happened if somebody got sick?

speaker: interviewee
text: Who went to a doctor in Europe? You had to be very, very sick to go to a doctor. My mother suffered from high blood pressure. So I remember her going to a doctor. Or, if you needed a doctor, you had to get a horse and buggy and travel 5 miles to [INAUDIBLE] and pick up the doctor and get him to the village. I remember the doctor came once. My mother was very sick with the high blood pressure. And he used leeches to-- to pull out the-- the bad blood. That's how they cured high blood pressure.

speaker: interviewer
text: So you witnessed this? You saw it?

speaker: interviewee
text: I witnessed it. I seen the leeches, yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: And did you start helping also as a very young child in the house?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. I used to. I was a spoiled-- spoiled brat. I was the youngest. And I sort of got away with murder. I didn't like to clean much. And later on, I helped my sister with the dressmaking. But in order to learn dressmaking, my sister gave me to do the bottoms, like hand work. And I right away wanted to work on the machine. So we had this disagreement. [LAUGHS]

speaker: interviewee
text: So I remember, I was about 9 or 10. And I learned to sew on the machine very fast. And I made myself a little dress. I remember my mother was bragging about it, that I was so young, and I made myself a little dress. Of course, the dress looked like a shirt, [LAUGHS] but the idea that I made it myself. And that your mom--

speaker: interviewee
text: So my--

speaker: interviewee
text: --complimented it, she felt proud.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. And later on, as I got older, I helped my sister with the sewing. And I attended school until 1939. I finished seventh grades, elementary school, which was equal to junior high school, I believe. Then when the Russians occupied our village, the-- many children went to work. I mean, they were already 14, 13, 14. So the Russians want to organize a sixth grade. And there weren't enough children.

speaker: interviewee
text: So the teacher came to ask my parents to ask me, would I come back to school. And I wasn't happy. It's like going backwards. But they explained that they teach more, which they did.

speaker: interviewee
text: They taught in the sixth grade more than the Polish school in the seventh grade. I remember they-- we had algebra, which I never had. And I enjoyed algebra very much, which we never had in the Polish seventh grade.

speaker: interviewer
text: So your classes in elementary school were solely conducted in Polish?

speaker: interviewee
text: In Polish. And I had some Ukrainian lessons. So I think twice a week, we had Ukrainian lessons.

speaker: interviewer
text: In the school?

speaker: interviewee
text: In the same school. Because the population was about half Polish and half Ukrainian.

speaker: interviewer
text: How-- how well did they live together, the Poles and the Ukrainians?

speaker: interviewee
text: They got along. There was quite-- in fact, we, the Jewish people, got along with the non-Jewish people, most of them. I mean, we heard some anti-Semitic remarks here and there. But not-- I remember when I was quite little, my mother wouldn't allow me to play with the non-Jewish children. Well, they used coarse language. And she was shielding me from the anti-Semitic remarks. So I just had a neighbor, a little girl that I used to play with as a little girl.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was she Jewish, the neighbor?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, no. She-- I mean, that neighbor my mother OKed for me to play with her. And then when I came to school, I-- that's the contact I had with the Polish and Ukrainian children. I remember in the fourth and fifth grade, I befriended a girl. Her father was an engineer. So she was an upper class, middle class. So my mother approved of the friendship.

speaker: interviewer
text: And the other one's mother approved, as well, the--

speaker: interviewee
text: Right, yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: So there wasn't a tremendous sense of anti-Semitism in the town?

speaker: interviewee
text: Not a tremendous, no. We sort of lived where there we got along. We got along.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were the Jews in another kind of clothing, in a different garb? Or the--

speaker: interviewee
text: Not really. My father wore a regulator suit. It was a little different. I mean, the peasants wore more, you know, plainer clothes. But my father used to wear a [NON-ENGLISH]-- do I have to explain it, [? a fore-hat-- ?] only on yontif, like kind of Rosh Hashanah in Hebrew. Saturday, I don't remember if he wore it on Saturday.

speaker: interviewee
text: So as far as dressing, it was not outrageously different that my father dressed. We tried to dress-- what was the difference? It was like, if somebody wore boots, so that was a sign of a [NON-ENGLISH], you know, a commoner. So I remember my mother never wore boots. She wore those high shoes. That was the style. And my father boots wintertime. But summertimes, he always wore shoes.

speaker: interviewee
text: I remember my sister was friends with a boy in our village. His name was Leon Halpern, [NON-ENGLISH] they used to call him. And they were really in love. For a few years, they were going out. But he couldn't get married. Because he had an older sister, a single sister.

speaker: interviewee
text: So my father, especially, was on top of it. My-- my daughter, he said, you're wasting-- you're wasting your life away. He's not going to get married, because his sister is not getting married. So my father wanted to introduce my sister to a  boy. So he invited those two boys. And two boys came that were friends or something. And they wore boots. And after they left, my sister says, you want me to marry those [NON-ENGLISH], those [INAUDIBLE]? [LAUGHS] Only because they wore boots. [LAUGHS] I mean, she didn't talk to them to find out how intelligent they are. But it just meant a lot. And in terms of the Jews in the town, in terms of professions, what were the similarities and differences between the townspeople that were not Jewish and the Jewish townspeople?

speaker: interviewee
text: Most non-Jewish people, the Polish and Ukrainians, were farmers. There was a shoemaker. There was a dressmaker. And the Jewish people had stores. They had grocery stores. There was one man, he sold leather, and also a grocer. Like, that one room, one corner was a grocery store, six shelves. And at another table he sold leather.

speaker: interviewee
text: And then he sold cigarettes, which you had to have a permit or something. So he was called the rich man. I made a painting of him. I have many paintings of this-- the life there. So there was a bit of a difference. Although, there was also-- they called it a cooperative store, a grocery run by the government, I believe, for the town, you know? Did they keep the prices at a certain-- what was-- what did it mean to be cooperative?

speaker: interviewee
text: It was just run by the townspeople. I think it was part of the government.

speaker: interviewer
text: Run by the government of the town itself?

speaker: interviewee
text: Of the government, yeah. Yeah. I believe so.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what crops did your father raise on his farm?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, he raised potatoes, and corn, wheat, and [NON-ENGLISH], you know, what you call--

speaker: interviewee
text: Groats.

speaker: interviewee
text: Oats. Oats.

speaker: interviewee
text: Groats. Groats.

speaker: interviewee
text: Groats. And oats, also, for the animals.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did he sell some of his products outside of the town?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. We used it mostly for ourselves. We made flour. My father would take the wheat. I mean, of course, the people would cut the wheat in the fields and bring it to the barn. And we used to hire the people to shred the wheat. This is-- was another thing. To work on the farm, to work in the fields, was something that if you worked in the fields, you were called a peasant. And if you-- now, we spent every penny we had to the peasants. But we didn't go to work in the fields. [LAUGHS] Because it was shunned. [LAUGHS]

speaker: interviewee
text: So we used to hire people to-- for all the work, shred the wheat. And then so we used to make the flour from the wheat for our use. And mostly we did it-- I don't really remember if he sold anything.

speaker: interviewee
text: I doubt it. It was for our own use. We didn't have too many-- too much land. Otherwise, we had a few-- we used to call it [NON-ENGLISH]. I don't know what is the equal here. [LAUGHS] They were like strips of land that we owned. In fact, the-- it made beautiful scenery to have a painting of that. The-- each area-- by the way, there were rich people. Now, they owned a lot of land. And they were called the [NON-ENGLISH], [NON-ENGLISH]. They owned the land around the house. And the land was all together in the same area. The poorer people had a strip of land to one corner of the town and a strip of land in another. Otherwise, if you had money, a few zlotys, you bought a strip of land, and that's how.

speaker: interviewee
text: And also, each area was known of growing a certain-- let's say, one area was-- they grew potatoes there. The potatoes were growing there. Another corner of the-- of the town, a strip of land, the wheat was growing better. Do you understand? So your father had this also, that he had to go from one end to the other?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. He had to travel, or walk. He-- he used to walk many times. Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: Describe the surroundings of Czernica. Were there woods around the town?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. There-- there were. The-- the-- the village was in the valley. There was the main road, a crossroad. And there was a small river flowing through the village. And there were small alleys going uphill. On both sides, there were hills. The farmers lived along the roads and up the hills, too. And each one had a garden. Each farm had a garden. I mean, we also had a garden, which is like an acre. That's what the word is. We had like a half acre of land around the house. And that was for vegetable. And each farmer had like that, acre, half acre land. And they growed-- grew their vegetable for their own use also. And they sold to the markets. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Then on the one side of the village, there were woods. And on the other side, there were fields. On two sides of the village, there were fields all around. So like a mile away from the village, the fields started. And it was beautiful scenery. I remember walking sometimes and admiring. My artist was [LAUGHS] waking up at the time to see those beautiful strips of land in different colors. You know, it was very nice.

speaker: interviewer
text: Give me an impression of a holiday in your house. It was an orthodox house.

speaker: interviewee
text: An orthodox house. My mother started baking Thursday, going to the grocery shopping. I mean, like we went for-- for five [INAUDIBLE] we put a little bit of cinnamon or sugar. I mean, just for each week, you know, we bought separately.

speaker: interviewer
text: So for the holiday, for the Sabbath and for holidays, you saved the whole week, the money, to make a nice meal for the Sabbath and for the holidays. So, like Wednesday-Thursday, my mother went shopping. And Thursday she baked the challah and the potato mix. We're talking about Saturday. Is that right? She baked huge, huge-- what do you call those things? Potato mix. Huge potato mix. And some like danishes, you would call now, for Saturday, for the the Shabbas.

speaker: interviewee
text: And then she would make fish and chicken soup and so forth and cholent many times. That's the-- it's like a stew. You put it in the oven Friday. And Saturday, for the noon meal it was ready. Now, for the holidays, there were more goodies. I remember for Rosh Hashanah she used to make some dough dipped in honey. This was-- this was a delicacy. And she used to make a gefilte fish and bake all kind of sponge cake, a honey cake that was for the holiday. And, of course, cooked the-- like a pot roast sometimes and chicken.

speaker: interviewer
text: Who came to your holidays? Did you have other people join you?

speaker: interviewee
text: We always were celebrating the our-- our own family. We-- somehow we didn't have the style of inviting people for the holidays, especially the Seder. We-- my father conducted the Seder just for our own family. That was the style. Somehow, at least in our town, we didn't invite family. We would give the donations. Would give money to the rabbi in order the poor people should be able to make their own Seder, to prepare their own food for the Seder.

speaker: interviewer
text: Wonderful. We'll stop this tape now. Thank you.
